title: 7 1 Laplace transforms
body:
TOPIC 7 : LAPLACE TRANSFORMS I
              7.1 LAPLACE TRANSFORMS


                     LAPLACE TRANSFORMS

    0




                                        1
 The Laplace transform is a very useful technique that allows us to transform a function
f (t) into another form F (s). The two forms of the function are completely equivalent and
we are free to move from one to the other as need be. As we shall see, the Laplace trans-
form is particularly effective in problems involving discontinuous changes in the function
f (t) and so is extremely useful in applications of electrical engineering where variables
(voltage, current, etc) can switch on or off instantaneously.

The definition on the Laplace transform is
                                                  Z∞
                             L{f (t)} = F (s) =        e−st f (t) dt
                                                  0

That is we integrate the function f against a decaying exponential function all the way
from 0 to ∞.

We denote the original function by f (t) and the transformed object F (s). To keep track
of where we are, we use t as the original variable and the transformed variable is s. Just
like differentiation and integration, it is a linear process so that

                        L {af (t) + bg(t)} = aL {f (t)} + bL {g(t)} .

In practice, we generally do not use the integral definition to calculate Laplace transforms.
Instead we will use a table of Laplace transforms (and their inverses). This table is printed
at the start of these lecture notes and in your tutorial problems. You will also have a
copy of this table in the exam.


But first a little revision on integration by parts and improper integrals.




                                             2
 Example 1 Evaluate each of the following definite integrals:
     R1
a)   0
          3te−7t dt.
     R∞
b)    0
           3te−7t dt.

One thing to remember is that the exponential function goes to zero much faster than
any polynomial goes to infinity!




                                                           3  24            3
                                                  F a)       − e−7     b)        F
                                                          49 49             49

                                         3
 Example 2 Find the Laplace transform of 4t, firstly via the definition and then by
using the table of Laplace transforms.




                                                                         4
                                                                     F        F
                                                                         s2

                                        4
 You can see from the above example that the Laplace transform is an improper integral
(since we are integrating all the way to infinity). What saves us is that e−st converges to
zero very aggressively and many of our standard functions (certainly all of the polynomi-
als, sin and cos) are quickly defeated by e−st . In general the Laplace transform of f will
exist provided that f does not grow too rapidly as t → ∞.

Observe also that the integral is with respect to t only, but the integrand involves both
s and t. Thus, the Laplace transform will depend on s. Notice also that we assume that
t ≥ 0. The variable t usually represents time so this quite natural. A nice thing about
Laplace transforms (as opposed to derivatives for example) is that they do not get too
worried when faced with discontinuities.

                          

 Example 5 Use the table to find the Laplace transform of

Note that the gamma function Γ is an extension of the concept of factorials beyond the
                                               R∞
positive integers. Its definition is Γ(ν + 1) = e−x xν dx and it is usually evaluated by
                                                   0
computer or tables. If ν is a positive integer then Γ(ν + 1) = n!


One final note is that the concept of an arbitrary constant C plays no role whatsoever in
the theory of Laplace transforms! All of the integrals are definite integrals, so there are
no unknown constants of integration.
